summary: When I entered the service in July 1944, I was living just outside Franklin Park, Illinois, where I had gone to school. I graduated high school on June 8, 1944, right before being drafted. Franklin Park was my home base before I left for military training and eventually overseas deployment.

I was drafted into the United States Army in 1944 after graduating high school. I chose the Army because my brother was already in it and I didn't want to join the Navy since I couldn't swim. My entire training and combat experience, including boot camp and overseas service, was with the Army.

I was inducted into the Army at Fort Sheridan, Illinois. It was my first time away from home, and like everyone else, I was excited to get new clothes and free meals. Fort Sheridan was where my military journey officially began.

My basic training took place at Camp Blanding, Florida. The instructors were veterans who taught us everything from rifles to machine guns and mortars. It was a warm place with lots of sand, very different from home, and I learned how to operate weapons and pass tough tests there.

After my training at Camp Blanding and a short furlough, I was sent to Camp Miles Standish near Boston, Massachusetts. From there, I boarded the troop ship Santa Rosa to head overseas. Camp Miles Standish was my last stop in the States before deployment.

I went to Camp Miles Standish near Boston after finishing my training in Florida. Boston was the area where we prepared for overseas deployment before boarding the ship. It marked the transition from training to active service abroad.

We boarded the Santa Rosa, a small cruise ship converted into a troop ship, around January 1, 1945. It took us from Boston to Le Havre, France, where we arrived as replacements for the front lines. The Santa Rosa was my passage across the Atlantic into the European theater.

We landed at Le Havre, France, in early January 1945 after crossing the Atlantic on the Santa Rosa. From there, we took a night train to Belgium to join our division. Le Havre was the entry point into the European combat zone for me.

I joined the 75th Infantry Division in the woods of Belgium in January 1945. It was cold and we ate canned food heated in boiling water. Belgium was where I first met my unit and prepared for combat operations in the region.

I was assigned to the 75th Infantry Division, specifically the 289th Infantry Regiment, First Battalion. This division became my new family in Europe as we fought in the Colmar Pocket and later moved into Germany and Holland. I served as a private and was promoted to Private First Class during my time with the 75th.

Within the 75th Infantry Division, I was part of the 289th Infantry Regiment, First Battalion, D Company, which handled heavy weapons like machine guns and mortars. We saw our first real combat near Colmar, France, in early 1945. The 289th was where I learned the realities of war and earned my Combat Infantryman Badge.

My first real battle was in the Colmar Pocket near where France, Germany, and Switzerland meet, around January 1945. We faced heavy artillery and mortar fire, and I helped wounded men after an 88 mm shell hit a house where we were setting up a machine gun. The battle was intense, and I was promoted and awarded for my service there.

The Colmar Pocket where I fought was close to the Swiss border, near where France and Germany also meet. Although Switzerland was neutral, the proximity to this border was a notable geographic point during our combat operations. We were operating in a tri-border area in early 1945.

After fighting in the Colmar Pocket in France, we pushed the German forces back toward their own territory. We eventually reached the edge of Germany before being sent north to Holland. Germany was the enemy's homeland we were advancing into during the final months of the war.

France was where much of my combat experience took place, especially around the Colmar Pocket near the German and Swiss borders. We operated alongside the French Army there, who had tanks supporting us. France was a key battleground in early 1945 before we moved into Germany and Holland.

After the battle in the Colmar Pocket, my unit was sent north to Holland to join forces with the British Army. We arrived there in February 1945, where the British had been stationed since October. Holland was the next phase of our operations as the war was drawing to a close.

In Holland, we joined the British Army, who had been holding their positions since October. They had built small wooden huts for shelter, which was quite different from our previous conditions. Working alongside the British was a new experience as we continued pushing the enemy back in early 1945.
